[[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Dai}})
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Sakamoto, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}} :* {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :* {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{jalink|Takeminakata||Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{jalink|Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{jalink|Kōman Ō|功満王}}, a remote ancestor of the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}} {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{jalink|Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{jalink|Chūai|Q179971}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}{{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336|Haibara District}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{jalink|Early modern period|Q5308718}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336}}{{jalink|Kawanehon Town|Q1204025}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{jalink|Yaizu City|Q696783}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{jalink|Yoshida Town|Q1202789}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{jalink|Ōi River|Q1044017}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 ({{jalink|1597|Q6790}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{jalink|Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{jalink|6th century|Q8090}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{jalink|National histories|Q11420601|Six National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) the {{jalink|court rank|Q11591025}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{jalink|Oguni Shrine|Q421597}} ({{jalink|Shūchi District|Q1205501}}{{jalink|Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|Mori Town}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{jalink|Medieval period|Q12554}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} era ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}}–{{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the lord of {{jalink|Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|Kakegawa Domain}}, {{jalink|Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, by order of the shogunate, {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} era ({{jalink|1688|Q7671}}–{{jalink|1704|Q6824}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{jalink|1911|Q2076}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 21 ({{jalink|1946|Q18610}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 3 ({{jalink|1832|Q7584}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{jalink|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230}} and {{jalink|Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289}} in {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{jalink|Nintoku|Q313119}} in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{jalink|Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Kinmei|Q329792}} ({{jalink|542|Q33631}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{jalink|zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{jalink|Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources''' [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Dai}})
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Sakamoto, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}} :* {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :* {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{jalink|Takeminakata||Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{jalink|Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{jalink|Kōman Ō|功満王}}, a remote ancestor of the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}} {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{jalink|Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{jalink|Chūai|Q179971}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}{{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336|Haibara District}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{jalink|Early modern period|Q5308718}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336}}{{jalink|Kawanehon Town|Q1204025}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{jalink|Yaizu City|Q696783}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{jalink|Yoshida Town|Q1202789}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{jalink|Ōi River|Q1044017}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 ({{jalink|1597|Q6790}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{jalink|Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{jalink|6th century|Q8090}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{jalink|National histories|Q11420601|Six National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) the {{jalink|court rank|Q11591025}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{jalink|Oguni Shrine|Q421597}} ({{jalink|Shūchi District|Q1205501}}{{jalink|Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|Mori Town}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{jalink|Medieval period|Q12554}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} era ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}}–{{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the lord of {{jalink|Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|Kakegawa Domain}}, {{jalink|Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, by order of the shogunate, {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} era ({{jalink|1688|Q7671}}–{{jalink|1704|Q6824}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{jalink|1911|Q2076}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 21 ({{jalink|1946|Q18610}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 3 ({{jalink|1832|Q7584}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{jalink|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230}} and {{jalink|Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289}} in {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{jalink|Nintoku|Q313119}} in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{jalink|Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Kinmei|Q329792}} ({{jalink|542|Q33631}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{jalink|zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{jalink|Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|group="Original"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Explanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine * Explanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine '''Books''' * Encyclopedias ** {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Keiichi Imai|chapter=Keiman Shrine|year=|title={{jalink|National History Encyclopedia (Shōwa period)|Q11420603|National History Encyclopedia}}|publisher={{jalink|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title={{jalink|Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Place Names|Q17213509}} 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference="Haibara District"|ref={{Harvid|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Keiman Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Taniguchi Kofun Group"|ref={{Harvid|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Compiled by the Meiji Shrine Records Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Keiman Shrine|title={{jalink|Meiji Shrine Records of Prefectural and County Shrines|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}}} *** [{{NDLDC|1088244/928}} *Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and County Shrines, Vol. 1*] (National Diet Library Digital Collections) see frames 928–929. ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1978|chapter=|title=History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|publisher=Shimada City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Compiled by the Shikinaisha Research Association|author=|chapter=|year=1988|title=Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha, Vol. 9|publisher={{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Ōkusu Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Keiman Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}. '''Websites''' * {{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/110601.html|author=|title=Keiman Shrine (Haibara District, Tōtōmi Province)|work=|publisher=Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection”|date=|accessdate=2015-7-8|ref={{Harvid|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Keiman-jinja}} * {{Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection|110601}} {{Shinto navigation}} {{Redirect category shell |redirect1=Ōkusu Shrine (Shimada City) |1-1=Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=History of Shimada City |1-3=Shikinaisha of Tōtōmi Province |1-4=Former village shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Keiman Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:島田市の歴史]] [[Category:島田市の建築物]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社|名けいまんしんしや]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:秦氏|社けいまんしんしや]]

* {'text': '静岡県島田市阪本4054-1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Keiman-jinja}} [[JA:敬満神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499585}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Keiman Shrine |Image = [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Dai}})
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Sakamoto, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}} :* {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :* {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{jalink|Takeminakata||Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{jalink|Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{jalink|Kōman Ō|功満王}}, a remote ancestor of the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}} {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{jalink|Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{jalink|Chūai|Q179971}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}{{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336|Haibara District}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{jalink|Early modern period|Q5308718}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336}}{{jalink|Kawanehon Town|Q1204025}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{jalink|Yaizu City|Q696783}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{jalink|Yoshida Town|Q1202789}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{jalink|Ōi River|Q1044017}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 ({{jalink|1597|Q6790}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{jalink|Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{jalink|6th century|Q8090}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{jalink|National histories|Q11420601|Six National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) the {{jalink|court rank|Q11591025}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{jalink|Oguni Shrine|Q421597}} ({{jalink|Shūchi District|Q1205501}}{{jalink|Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|Mori Town}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{jalink|Medieval period|Q12554}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} era ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}}–{{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the lord of {{jalink|Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|Kakegawa Domain}}, {{jalink|Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, by order of the shogunate, {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} era ({{jalink|1688|Q7671}}–{{jalink|1704|Q6824}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{jalink|1911|Q2076}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 21 ({{jalink|1946|Q18610}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 3 ({{jalink|1832|Q7584}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{jalink|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230}} and {{jalink|Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289}} in {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{jalink|Nintoku|Q313119}} in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{jalink|Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Kinmei|Q329792}} ({{jalink|542|Q33631}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{jalink|zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{jalink|Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources''' [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Dai}})
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Sakamoto, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}} :* {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :* {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{jalink|Takeminakata||Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{jalink|Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{jalink|Kōman Ō|功満王}}, a remote ancestor of the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}} {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{jalink|Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{jalink|Chūai|Q179971}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}{{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336|Haibara District}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{jalink|Early modern period|Q5308718}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336}}{{jalink|Kawanehon Town|Q1204025}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{jalink|Yaizu City|Q696783}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{jalink|Yoshida Town|Q1202789}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{jalink|Ōi River|Q1044017}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 ({{jalink|1597|Q6790}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{jalink|Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{jalink|6th century|Q8090}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{jalink|National histories|Q11420601|Six National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) the {{jalink|court rank|Q11591025}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{jalink|Oguni Shrine|Q421597}} ({{jalink|Shūchi District|Q1205501}}{{jalink|Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|Mori Town}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{jalink|Medieval period|Q12554}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} era ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}}–{{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the lord of {{jalink|Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|Kakegawa Domain}}, {{jalink|Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, by order of the shogunate, {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} era ({{jalink|1688|Q7671}}–{{jalink|1704|Q6824}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{jalink|1911|Q2076}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 21 ({{jalink|1946|Q18610}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 3 ({{jalink|1832|Q7584}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{jalink|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230}} and {{jalink|Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289}} in {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{jalink|Nintoku|Q313119}} in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{jalink|Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Kinmei|Q329792}} ({{jalink|542|Q33631}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{jalink|zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{jalink|Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources''' [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Dai}})
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 15|Q2919}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Sakamoto, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}} :* {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :* {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{jalink|Takeminakata||Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{jalink|Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{jalink|Kōman Ō|功満王}}, a remote ancestor of the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{jalink|Qin|Q34756}} {{jalink|First Emperor|Q7192}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{jalink|Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{jalink|Chūai|Q179971}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}{{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336|Haibara District}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{jalink|Early modern period|Q5308718}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 13 ({{jalink|1938|Q18645}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{jalink|Haibara District|Q1203336}}{{jalink|Kawanehon Town|Q1204025}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{jalink|Yaizu City|Q696783}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{jalink|Yoshida Town|Q1202789}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{jalink|Ōi River|Q1044017}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} 2 ({{jalink|1597|Q6790}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{jalink|Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{jalink|6th century|Q8090}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{jalink|National histories|Q11420601|Six National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) the {{jalink|court rank|Q11591025}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{jalink|Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{jalink|Oguni Shrine|Q421597}} ({{jalink|Shūchi District|Q1205501}}{{jalink|Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|Mori Town}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, compiled in {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{jalink|Medieval period|Q12554}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} era ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}}–{{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the lord of {{jalink|Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|Kakegawa Domain}}, {{jalink|Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, by order of the shogunate, {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} era ({{jalink|1688|Q7671}}–{{jalink|1704|Q6824}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{jalink|1874|Q7761}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{jalink|1911|Q2076}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 21 ({{jalink|1946|Q18610}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{jalink|Ninju|Q1373077}} 3 ({{jalink|853|Q30367}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 2 ({{jalink|860|Q24714}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 3 ({{jalink|1832|Q7584}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{jalink|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230}} and {{jalink|Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289}} in {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{jalink|Nintoku|Q313119}} in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{jalink|Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Kinmei|Q329792}} ({{jalink|542|Q33631}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{jalink|Shimada City|Q605256}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{jalink|zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{jalink|Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources'''
