category|Category:Keiman-jinja}} [[JA:敬満神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499585}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Keiman Shrine |Image = [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Dai|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|qid=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Sakamoto, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} :* {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{ill|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|UNKNOWN|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} – Enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|qid=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōman Ō|1=UNKNOWN}}, a remote ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|qid=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{ill|lt=Qin|Q34756|qid=Q34756|1=Qin (state)}} {{ill|lt=First Emperor|Q7192|qid=Q7192|1=Qin Shi Huang}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{ill|lt=Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250|qid=Q11066250|1=Yuzuki no Kimi}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Chūai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}{{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|lt=Haibara District|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1938|Q18645|qid=Q18645|1=1938}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawanehon Town|Q1204025|qid=Q1204025|1=Kawanehon}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yaizu City|Q696783|qid=Q696783|1=Yaizu}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{ill|lt=Yoshida Town|Q1202789|qid=Q1202789|1=Yoshida, Shizuoka}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{ill|lt=Ōi River|Q1044017|qid=Q1044017|1=Ōi River}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1597|Q6790|qid=Q6790|1=1597}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{ill|lt=Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346|qid=Q11570346|1=Makinohara Plateau}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|qid=Q8090|1=6th century}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=National histories|Q11420601|qid=Q11420601|lt=Six National Histories|1=National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) the {{ill|lt=court rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{ill|lt=Oguni Shrine|Q421597|qid=Q421597|1=Okuni Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Shūchi District|Q1205501|qid=Q1205501|1=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|qid=Q1345571|lt=Mori Town|1=Mori, Shizuoka}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{ill|lt=Medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}–{{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|qid=Q6772|1=1593}}), the lord of {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|qid=Q3034456|lt=Kakegawa Domain|1=Kakegawa Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878|qid=Q1315878|1=Yamauchi Kazutoyo}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, by order of the shogunate, {{ill|lt=Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913|qid=Q3387913|1=Ina Tadatsugu}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|qid=Q7671|1=1688}}–{{ill|lt=1704|Q6824|qid=Q6824|1=1704}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|qid=Q2076|1=1911}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|qid=Q18610|1=1946}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|qid=Q7584|1=1832}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{ill|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230|qid=Q11437230|1=Oarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289|qid=Q11644289|1=Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Nintoku|Q313119|qid=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}} in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{ill|lt=Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|qid=Q55406552|lt=Yamato no Agoko|1=Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|qid=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Kinmei|Q329792|qid=Q329792|1=Emperor Kinmei}} ({{ill|lt=542|Q33631|qid=Q33631|1=542}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{ill|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{ill|lt=Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|qid=Q4304536|lt=Hōrai Bridge|1=Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|group="Original"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === Further reading === * Explanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine * Explanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine '''Books''' * Encyclopedias ** {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Keiichi Imai|chapter=Keiman Shrine|year=|title={{ill|lt=National History Encyclopedia (Shōwa period)|Q11420603|qid=Q11420603|lt=National History Encyclopedia|1=Kokushi Daijiten}}|publisher={{ill|lt=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|Q4346755|qid=Q4346755|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Place Names|Q17213509|qid=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|qid=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference="Haibara District"|ref={{Harvid|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Keiman Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Taniguchi Kofun Group"|ref={{Harvid|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Compiled by the Meiji Shrine Records Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Keiman Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Records of Prefectural and County Shrines|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}}} *** [{{NDLDC|1088244/928}} *Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and County Shrines, Vol. 1*] (National Diet Library Digital Collections) see frames 928–929. ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1978|chapter=|title=History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|publisher=Shimada City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Compiled by the Shikinaisha Research Association|author=|chapter=|year=1988|title=Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha, Vol. 9|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|qid=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Ōkusu Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Keiman Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}. '''Websites''' * {{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/110601.html|author=|title=Keiman Shrine (Haibara District, Tōtōmi Province)|work=|publisher=Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection”|date=|accessdate=2015-7-8|ref={{Harvid|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Keiman-jinja}} * {{Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection|110601}} {{Shinto navigation}} {{Redirect category shell |redirect1=Ōkusu Shrine (Shimada City) |1-1=Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=History of Shimada City |1-3=Shikinaisha of Tōtōmi Province |1-4=Former village shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Keiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Keiman Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shimada]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shimada]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社|名けいまんしんしや]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hata clan]]

category|Category:Keiman-jinja}} [[JA:敬満神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499585}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Keiman Shrine |Image = [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Dai|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|qid=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Sakamoto, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} :* {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{ill|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|UNKNOWN|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} – Enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|qid=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōman Ō|1=UNKNOWN}}, a remote ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|qid=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{ill|lt=Qin|Q34756|qid=Q34756|1=Qin (state)}} {{ill|lt=First Emperor|Q7192|qid=Q7192|1=Qin Shi Huang}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{ill|lt=Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250|qid=Q11066250|1=Yuzuki no Kimi}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Chūai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}{{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|lt=Haibara District|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1938|Q18645|qid=Q18645|1=1938}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawanehon Town|Q1204025|qid=Q1204025|1=Kawanehon}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yaizu City|Q696783|qid=Q696783|1=Yaizu}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{ill|lt=Yoshida Town|Q1202789|qid=Q1202789|1=Yoshida, Shizuoka}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{ill|lt=Ōi River|Q1044017|qid=Q1044017|1=Ōi River}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1597|Q6790|qid=Q6790|1=1597}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{ill|lt=Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346|qid=Q11570346|1=Makinohara Plateau}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|qid=Q8090|1=6th century}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=National histories|Q11420601|qid=Q11420601|lt=Six National Histories|1=National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) the {{ill|lt=court rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{ill|lt=Oguni Shrine|Q421597|qid=Q421597|1=Okuni Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Shūchi District|Q1205501|qid=Q1205501|1=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|qid=Q1345571|lt=Mori Town|1=Mori, Shizuoka}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{ill|lt=Medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}–{{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|qid=Q6772|1=1593}}), the lord of {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|qid=Q3034456|lt=Kakegawa Domain|1=Kakegawa Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878|qid=Q1315878|1=Yamauchi Kazutoyo}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, by order of the shogunate, {{ill|lt=Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913|qid=Q3387913|1=Ina Tadatsugu}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|qid=Q7671|1=1688}}–{{ill|lt=1704|Q6824|qid=Q6824|1=1704}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|qid=Q2076|1=1911}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|qid=Q18610|1=1946}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|qid=Q7584|1=1832}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{ill|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230|qid=Q11437230|1=Oarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289|qid=Q11644289|1=Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Nintoku|Q313119|qid=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}} in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{ill|lt=Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|qid=Q55406552|lt=Yamato no Agoko|1=Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|qid=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Kinmei|Q329792|qid=Q329792|1=Emperor Kinmei}} ({{ill|lt=542|Q33631|qid=Q33631|1=542}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{ill|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{ill|lt=Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|qid=Q4304536|lt=Hōrai Bridge|1=Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes === District"|ref={{Harvid|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Keiman Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Taniguchi Kofun Group"|ref={{Harvid|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}}}. * Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Compiled by the Meiji Shrine Records Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Keiman Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Records of Prefectural and County Shrines|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Records Editorial frames 928–929. ** {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1978|chapter=|title=History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|publisher=Shimada City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978}}}} ** University|Q1797584|qid=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Ōkusu Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Hiroshi Era “Keiman Shrine”|ref={{Harvid|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}}}. '''Websites''' * {{Cite web|Japanese|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/110601.html|author=|title=Keiman Shrine (Haibara District, Tōtōmi Province)|work=|publisher=Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection”|date=|accessdate=2015-7-8|ref={{Harvid|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Keiman-jinja}} * {{Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection|110601}} {{Shinto navigation}} {{Redirect category shell |redirect1=Ōkusu Shrine (Shimada City) |1-1=Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture |1-2=History of Shimada City |1-3=Shikinaisha of Tōtōmi Province |1-4=Former village shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture }} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Keiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Keiman Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shimada]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shimada]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社|名けいまんしんしや]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hata clan]]

category|Category:Keiman-jinja}} [[JA:敬満神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499585}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Keiman Shrine |Image = [[File:Keiman-jinja haiden.JPG|280px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|34|49|3.06|N|138|12|13.8|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}} Sakamoto 4054-1 |ISO = JP-22 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 49|Latitude seconds = 3.06 |Longitude degrees = 138|Longitude minutes = 12|Longitude seconds = 13.80 |Enshrined deity = Keiman-no-Kami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Dai|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 26th year of the reign of the 11th Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|qid=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} |Rituals = |Label position = top |Map = Japan Shizuoka }} [[File:Keiman-jinja entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Keiman Shrine''' (Keiman Jinja[http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4407178 Keiman Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines). / Kyōman Jinja{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Sakamoto, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following nine kamiExplanatory board within the grounds of Keiman Shrine.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records|1912}}. ; Principal deity :* '''Keiman-no-Kami''' (Kyōman-shin / Keiman-shin) ; Associated deities :* {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} :* {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} ; Enshrined together :* {{ill|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|UNKNOWN|qid=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} – Enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}) as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} – Likewise enshrined as the deity of Suwa Shrine. :* {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Akutsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Atago Shrine. :* Miketsu-kami – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Sankō Shrine. :* {{ill|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} – Enshrined in the same year as the deity of Tenjin Shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3., the deity appears as “Keiman Shinrei,” but since {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|qid=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} argued in his *Banshinkō* that the deity should be identified with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōman Ō|1=UNKNOWN}}, a remote ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}}, this theory has become known{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. According to the {{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|qid=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}Shinsen Shōjiroku, Left Capital, Various Foreigners, Han, Uzumasa no Kimi Sukune entry., Kōman Ō is a legendary figure said to be a son of Emperor Kōbu, a third-generation descendant of the {{ill|lt=Qin|Q34756|qid=Q34756|1=Qin (state)}} {{ill|lt=First Emperor|Q7192|qid=Q7192|1=Qin Shi Huang}}, and the father of Yuzuki no Kimi ({{ill|lt=Yuzuki no Kimi|Q11066250|qid=Q11066250|1=Yuzuki no Kimi}}), and to have immigrated in the 8th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Chūai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}} (however, the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Gankei 7 (883) Dec. 25.Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Ninna 3 (887) July 17. records differing traditions regarding origin and time of immigration). Under the Kōman Ō theory, “Keiman” is equated with “Kōman,” and based on the existence of “Hata no Kuroshige,” recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}Shoku Nihon Kōki, Jōwa 14 (847) Aug. 17. as a person of {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}{{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|lt=Haibara District|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}, it is hypothesized that the immigrant Hata clan who lived in this area enshrined their clan deity here{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. From the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, the enshrined deity came to be recorded in documents as {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}, and this theory was followed even after the Meiji era; however, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1938|Q18645|qid=Q18645|1=1938}}) it was corrected to the present “Keiman-no-Kami”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the surrounding area to the present day, the distribution of the following three related shrines is known: * Keiman Ōi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Haibara District|Q1203336|qid=Q1203336|1=Haibara District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kawanehon Town|Q1204025|qid=Q1204025|1=Kawanehon}} Senzu) * Kagami-man Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yaizu City|Q696783|qid=Q696783|1=Yaizu}} Nishijima) * Keiman Shrine (enshrined together at Hachiman Shrine in Ōhata, {{ill|lt=Yoshida Town|Q1202789|qid=Q1202789|1=Yoshida, Shizuoka}}, Haibara District) These are said to be branch shrines from Keiman Shrine (this shrine){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the founding dates to the 26th year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} (the 11th emperor), but details are unclear. As noted above, there is a theory that it began with the enshrinement of the clan deity by the immigrant {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|qid=Q987290|1=Hata clan}} who lived in the area{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. The changes in the location of enshrinement are also unclear. *Shinmyōchō Kōshō* states that it was originally near the {{ill|lt=Ōi River|Q1044017|qid=Q1044017|1=Ōi River}} but was moved to the present location due to flooding{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Ridgepole tags indicate that it had already been enshrined at the current site by at least {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1597|Q6790|qid=Q6790|1=1597}}){{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. On the other hand, the discovery of a sutra mound dating to around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}} about 90 meters north of the precinct suggests that the ancient site of enshrinement may also have been near the present location{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. Around Keiman Shrine, the distribution of the Taniguchihara Kofun Group, including the Atagozuka Kofun (a designated historic site of Shimada City), is known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. This kofun group is a late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} group said to have once consisted of more than 100 mounds, but the area, and even the {{ill|lt=Makinohara Plateau|Q11570346|qid=Q11570346|1=Makinohara Plateau}}, is a region where few sites or kofun from the Yayoi period to the early Kofun period are known{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Because construction of the kofun suddenly began around the mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|qid=Q8090|1=6th century}}, archaeologically it is assumed that immigrant groups different from the traditional local powers on the north bank of the Ōi River (the opposite bank) advanced into the area{{Sfn|Taniguchi Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Although the location of the district office of Haibara District in {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}} is unknown, there is a theory that it was near Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Haibara District (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=National histories|Q11420601|qid=Q11420601|lt=Six National Histories|1=National Histories}}, there is an entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) “Keiman Shinrei” was granted the status of a Myōjin, and another entry stating that in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) the {{ill|lt=court rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of “Keiman-no-Kami” was promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Within {{ill|lt=Tōtōmi Province|Q1048864|qid=Q1048864|1=Tōtōmi Province}}, this rank surpasses even that of {{ill|lt=Oguni Shrine|Q421597|qid=Q421597|1=Okuni Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Shūchi District|Q1205501|qid=Q1205501|1=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Mori Town, Shizuoka|Q1345571|qid=Q1345571|lt=Mori Town|1=Mori, Shizuoka}}, the province’s Ichinomiya) and Meharakawachi Shrine (two shrines proposed by identification theory), making it the highest rank. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it is listed as “Keiman Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in Haibara District of Tōtōmi Province, placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the same register, the reading of “Keiman” is given as “Kyōmano” or “Kyōman”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. Subsequent developments during the {{ill|lt=Medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} are unclear{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=155-159}}. During the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}–{{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|qid=Q6772|1=1593}}), the lord of {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|qid=Q3034456|lt=Kakegawa Domain|1=Kakegawa Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Kazutoyo|Q1315878|qid=Q1315878|1=Yamauchi Kazutoyo}}, is said to have donated shrine lands{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, by order of the shogunate, {{ill|lt=Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913|qid=Q3387913|1=Ina Tadatsugu}} also donated shrine lands, and during the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|qid=Q7671|1=1688}}–{{ill|lt=1704|Q6824|qid=Q6824|1=1704}}) 6 koku and 3 to were donated as exempt land{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Around this time, it was also called “Keiman Daibosatsu (Kagami-man Daibosatsu)”{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}), nearby Suwa Shrine, Atago Shrine, Sankō Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine were enshrined together{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|qid=Q2076|1=1911}}), it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|qid=Q18610|1=1946}}) January, it was recognized as qualifying for prefectural shrine status{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}, but the shrine rank system was abolished in February of that year. === Court ranks === * {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3 ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}) November 27: Granted Myōjin status (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku, Ninju 3 (853) November 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman Shinrei.” * {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 2 ({{ill|lt=860|Q24714|qid=Q24714|1=860}}) January 27: Promoted from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*)Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku, Jōgan 2 (860) January 27 (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection}}). – Recorded as “Keiman-no-Kami.” === Precincts === [[File:Keiman-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Main hall}}]] The main hall was constructed in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1832|Q7584|qid=Q7584|1=1832}}){{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (National History)}}. It is an ikken-sha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. The worship hall is in the {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, also with a copper-sheeted roof{{Sfn|Keiman Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the precincts''' * Water Deity Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} '''Shrines outside the precincts''' [[File:Okusu-jinja (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Ōkusu Shrine}}]] * Ōkusu Shrine (Ōkusu Jinja) ** Location: Sakamoto, Shimada City ({{Coord|34|49|3.57|N|138|12|27.29|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=Outside shrine: Ōkusu Shrine}}) ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}} ** Shrine rank: Shikinaisha “Ōkusu Shrine,” former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} *: It is enshrined about 350 meters east of Keiman Shrine. Regarding the enshrined deities, *Tōtōmi Province Fudoki-den* compares Ōkusu Shrine and Keiman Shrine to {{ill|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|Q11437230|qid=Q11437230|1=Oarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Sakurai Isosaki Shrine|Q11644289|qid=Q11644289|1=Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, respectively, proposing a theory that the former’s deity corresponds to Ōnamuchi-no-Kami (Ōkuninushi) and the latter’s to Sukunahikona-no-Kami{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. *: For Ōkusu Shrine, a connection has been suggested with the entry for the 62nd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Nintoku|Q313119|qid=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}} in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}Nihon Shoki, Emperor Nintoku, year 62, May.. In that entry, during a report by the governor of Tōtōmi Province, it is said that a great tree flowed down the Ōi River and stopped at a bend in the river, so {{ill|lt=Yamato no Agoko|Q55406552|qid=Q55406552|lt=Yamato no Agoko|1=Yamato no Agoko}} was dispatched to build a boat and transport it to Naniwa Harbor. The “great tree” mentioned is thought to be a {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|qid=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}}, and it is conjectured that Ōkusu Shrine enshrines its divine spiritExplanatory board within the grounds of Ōkusu Shrine.{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. According to the *Wamyōshō*, the existence of “Funaki-gō” in Haibara District of Suruga Province suggests the possibility that the area was an ancient shipbuilding site{{Sfn|History of Shimada City, Vol. 1|1978|pp=153-155}}. *: According to the shrine tradition of Ōkusu Shrine, it was founded in the 3rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Kinmei|Q329792|qid=Q329792|1=Emperor Kinmei}} ({{ill|lt=542|Q33631|qid=Q33631|1=542}}?){{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In the *Engishiki* Register of Deities, it is listed as a Shikinaisha small shrine in the same Haibara District as Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. In Meiji 6 (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in Meiji 44 (1911) it was merged with Keiman Shrine and became an outside shrine of Keiman Shrine{{Sfn|Ōkusu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1988}}. Today, only a small sanctuary remains. === On-site information === '''Address''' * Sakamoto 4054-1, {{ill|lt=Shimada City|Q605256|qid=Q605256|1=Shimada, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' [[File:Atagozuka Kofun (Shimada).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Atagozuka Kofun
(Designated historic site of Shimada City)}}]] * Taniguchihara Kofun Group ** Atagozuka Kofun – A {{ill|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|Q11268718|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} with a mound length of 21.5 meters, the largest in the Taniguchihara Kofun Group; designated historic site of Shimada City. * {{ill|lt=Hōrai Bridge (Ōi River)|Q4304536|qid=Q4304536|lt=Hōrai Bridge|1=Hōrai Bridge}} === Notes ===
